Question: Suppose the acceleration function has the form $$\mathbf{\vec{a}} ( t ) = a \mathbf{\hat{i}} + b \mathbf{\hat{j}} + c \mathbf{\hat{k}} \text{m/} \text{s}^{2} ,$$ where a, b, and c are constants. What can be said about the functional form of the velocity function?
Answer: The acceleration vector is constant and doesn’t change with time. If a, b , and c are not zero, then the velocity function must be linear in time. We have $$\mathbf{\vec{v}} ( t ) = \int \mathbf{\vec{a}} d t = \int ( a \mathbf{\hat{i}} + b \mathbf{\hat{j}} + c \mathbf{\hat{k}} ) d t = ( a \mathbf{\hat{i}} + b \mathbf{\hat{j}} + c \mathbf{\hat{k}} ) t \text{m/s} ,$$ since taking the derivative of the velocity function produces $$\mathbf{\vec{a}} ( t ) .$$ If any of the components of the acceleration are zero, then that component of the velocity would be a constant.
